title: West African Yassa Chicken (Senegalese Lemon-Onion Chicken)

subs.name: Bone-in fish steaks for a pescatarian yassa; reduce braising time to 10 minutes

ingredients.name: onion, halved and very thinly sliced
visual_checks: The onion is the star of yassa; do not reduce the quantity
ingredients.notes: The onion is the star of yassa; do not reduce the quantity

subs.name: Do not reduce quantity — yassa is specifically an onion-forward dish; shallots can replace half the onion for extra sweetness
Lime juice in the same quantity for a slightly brighter, more tropical finish

ingredients.name: mustard seeds, dry-roasted and ground into paste
visual_checks: Dijon mustard (2 tsp) is traditional; ground mustard seeds are the best Indian pantry substitute
ingredients.notes: Dijon mustard (2 tsp) is traditional; ground mustard seeds are the best Indian pantry substitute

subs.name: Prepared yellow mustard (1 tsp) or Dijon mustard (1.5 tsp) if available; omit entirely for a mustard-free lemon-onion chicken

steps.text: Combine lemon juice, ground mustard seeds, garlic, black pepper, green chilli and a generous amount of salt in a large bowl. Add the chicken pieces and all the sliced onions; toss everything together to coat and marinate for at least 30 minutes — up to 4 hours in the fridge gives deeper flavour.

Lift the chicken pieces from the marinade and reserve all the onions and marinade liquid. Heat 2 tbsp oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over high heat and sear the chicken for 3–4 minutes per side until nicely browned on the surface. Remove and set aside.

Reduce heat to medium-low. Add remaining 2 tbsp oil to the pot. Add all the reserved marinated onions and cook slowly, stirring occasionally, for 25 minutes until completely collapsed, deep caramel-brown and almost jammy — the mass will reduce to about a third of its original volume. This long caramelisation is the soul of yassa.

Return the seared chicken to the pot and nestle into the caramelised onions. Pour in all the reserved marinade liquid and add bay leaves. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer, cover and cook for 20 minutes until the chicken is tender. Taste for salt and remove bay leaves.

The finished yassa should be tangy and savoury with a thick onion sauce coating every piece of chicken. Scatter coriander leaves over the top and serve over plain white rice — the sauce soaks into the rice beautifully.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mustard-seeds → Prepared yellow mustard (1 tsp) or Dijon mustard (1.5 tsp) if available; omit entirely for a mustard-free lemon-onion chicken; chicken → Bone-in fish steaks for a pescatarian yassa; reduce braising time to 10 minutes; lemon → Lime juice in the same quantity for a slightly brighter, more tropical finish; onion → Do not reduce quantity — yassa is specifically an onion-forward dish; shallots can replace half the onion for extra sweetness
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Dry-roast mustard seeds (rai / kadugu) and grind into a coarse paste using a mortar and pestle — this replicates the mustard note exactly. The slow onion caramelisation is identical to the technique for onion-based Indian gravies. The entire dish cooks in a kadai. Serve over basmati rice.
